**This article contains [Persian text](/wiki/Persian_alphabet "Persian alphabet").** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Arabic "Help:Arabic"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)").

**Persian**,[[a]](#cite_note-12) also known by its [endonym](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") **Farsi**,[[b]](#cite_note-13) is a [Western Iranian language](/wiki/Western_Iranian_languages "Western Iranian languages") belonging to the [Iranian branch](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages") of the [Indo-Iranian subdivision](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_languages "Indo-Iranian languages") of the [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). Persian is a [pluricentric language](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language") predominantly spoken and used officially within [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), and [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") in three [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") [standard varieties](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"), respectively [Iranian Persian](/wiki/Iranian_Persian "Iranian Persian") (officially known as *Persian*),[[12]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-ISO-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) [Dari Persian](/wiki/Dari "Dari") (officially known as *Dari* since 1964),[[15]](#cite_note-Olesen-17) and [Tajiki Persian](/wiki/Tajik_language "Tajik language") (officially known as *Tajik* since 1999).[[16]](#cite_note-siddikzoda2002-18)[[17]](#cite_note-Baker-19) It is also spoken natively in the Tajik variety by a significant population within [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"),[[2]](#cite_note-auto-2)[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21) as well as within other regions with a [Persianate](/wiki/Persianate_society "Persianate society") history in the cultural sphere of [Greater Iran](/wiki/Greater_Iran "Greater Iran"). It is written officially within Iran and Afghanistan in the [Persian alphabet](/wiki/Persian_alphabet "Persian alphabet"), a derivative of the [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script"), and within Tajikistan in the [Tajik alphabet](/wiki/Tajik_alphabet "Tajik alphabet"), a derivative of the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script").

Modern Persian is a continuation of [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian"), an official language of the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire") (224–651 CE), itself a continuation of [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian "Old Persian"), which was used in the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") (550–330 BCE).[[20]](#cite_note-Lazard-22)[[21]](#cite_note-23) It originated in the region of [Fars](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province") ([Persia](/wiki/Persis "Persis")) in southwestern Iran.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

Throughout history, Persian was considered prestigious by various empires centered in [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"), [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia").[[23]](#cite_note-Persian_literature-25) Old Persian is attested in [Old Persian cuneiform](/wiki/Old_Persian_cuneiform "Old Persian cuneiform") on inscriptions from between the 6th and 4th century BC. Middle Persian is attested in [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic_alphabet "Aramaic alphabet")-derived scripts ([Pahlavi](/wiki/Pahlavi_scripts "Pahlavi scripts") and [Manichaean](/wiki/Manichaean_script "Manichaean script")) on [inscriptions](/wiki/Inscriptional_Pahlavi "Inscriptional Pahlavi") and in [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism") and [Manichaean](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism") scriptures from between the third to the tenth centuries (see [Middle Persian literature](/wiki/Middle_Persian_literature "Middle Persian literature")). New Persian literature was first recorded in the ninth century, after the [Muslim conquest of Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia"), since then adopting the Perso-Arabic script.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

Persian was the first language to break through the monopoly of [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") on writing in the [Muslim world](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world"), with [Persian poetry](/wiki/Persian_literature "Persian literature") becoming a tradition in many eastern courts.[[23]](#cite_note-Persian_literature-25) It was used officially as a language of bureaucracy even by non-native speakers, such as the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"),[[25]](#cite_note-27) the [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") in South Asia, and the [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") in Afghanistan. It influenced languages spoken in neighboring regions and beyond, including other Iranian languages, the [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages"), [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"), [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") and [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"). It also exerted some influence on Arabic,[[26]](#cite_note-Holes2001-28) while borrowing a lot of vocabulary from it in the Middle Ages.[[20]](#cite_note-Lazard-22)[[27]](#cite_note-Lazard,_Gilbert_1971-29)[[28]](#cite_note-Nushin_Namazi-30)[[29]](#cite_note-Classe_2000_1057-31)[[30]](#cite_note-lambtonexcerpt-32)

Some of the world's most famous pieces of literature from the Middle Ages, such as the *[Shahnameh](/wiki/Shahnameh "Shahnameh")* by [Ferdowsi](/wiki/Ferdowsi "Ferdowsi"), the works of [Rumi](/wiki/Rumi "Rumi"), the *[Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám](/wiki/Rubaiyat_of_Omar_Khayyam "Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam")*, the *[Panj Ganj](/wiki/Khamsa_of_Nizami "Khamsa of Nizami")* of [Nizami Ganjavi](/wiki/Nizami_Ganjavi "Nizami Ganjavi"), [*The Divān* of Hafez](/wiki/The_Div%C4%81n_of_Hafez "The Divān of Hafez"), *[The Conference of the Birds](/wiki/The_Conference_of_the_Birds "The Conference of the Birds")* by [Attar of Nishapur](/wiki/Attar_of_Nishapur "Attar of Nishapur"), and the miscellanea of *[Gulistan](/wiki/Gulistan_(book) "Gulistan (book)")* and *[Bustan](/wiki/Bustan_(book) "Bustan (book)")* by [Saadi Shirazi](/wiki/Saadi_Shirazi "Saadi Shirazi"), are written in Persian.[[31]](#cite_note-33) Some of the prominent modern Persian poets were [Nima Yooshij](/wiki/Nima_Yooshij "Nima Yooshij"), [Ahmad Shamlou](/wiki/Ahmad_Shamlou "Ahmad Shamlou"), [Simin Behbahani](/wiki/Simin_Behbahani "Simin Behbahani"), [Sohrab Sepehri](/wiki/Sohrab_Sepehri "Sohrab Sepehri"), [Rahi Mo'ayyeri](/wiki/Rahi_Mo%27ayyeri "Rahi Mo'ayyeri"), [Mehdi Akhavan-Sales](/wiki/Mehdi_Akhavan-Sales "Mehdi Akhavan-Sales"), and [Forugh Farrokhzad](/wiki/Forugh_Farrokhzad "Forugh Farrokhzad").

There are approximately 130 million Persian speakers worldwide, including [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians"), [Lurs](/wiki/Lurs "Lurs"), [Tajiks](/wiki/Tajiks "Tajiks"), [Hazaras](/wiki/Hazaras "Hazaras"), [Iranian Azeris](/wiki/Iranian_Azerbaijanis "Iranian Azerbaijanis"), [Iranian Kurds](/wiki/Iranian_Kurds "Iranian Kurds"), [Baloches](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people"), [Tats](/wiki/Tat_people_(Caucasus) "Tat people (Caucasus)"), [Afghan Pashtuns](/wiki/Afghan_Pashtuns "Afghan Pashtuns"), and [Aimaqs](/wiki/Aimaq_people "Aimaq people"). The term *Persophone* might also be used to refer to a speaker of Persian.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry2005284-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35)

## Classification

Persian is a member of the [Western Iranian group](/wiki/Western_Iranian_languages "Western Iranian languages") of the [Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages"), which make up a branch of the [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") in their [Indo-Iranian subdivision](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_languages "Indo-Iranian languages"). The Western Iranian languages themselves are divided into two subgroups: Southwestern Iranian languages, of which Persian is the most widely spoken, and Northwestern Iranian languages, of which [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_languages "Kurdish languages") and [Balochi](/wiki/Balochi_language "Balochi language") are the most widely spoken.[[34]](#cite_note-windfuhr1987-36)

## Name

The term *Persian* is an English derivation of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *Persiānus*, the adjectival form of *Persia*, itself deriving from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") *Persís* (Περσίς),[[35]](#cite_note-37) a Hellenized form of [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian "Old Persian") *Pārsa* (𐎱𐎠𐎼𐎿),[[36]](#cite_note-38) which means "[Persia](/wiki/Persis "Persis")" (a region in southwestern Iran, corresponding to modern-day [Fars](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province")). According to the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, the term *Persian* as a language name is first attested in English in the mid-16th century.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

**Farsi**, which is the Persian word for the Persian language, has also been used widely in English in recent decades, more often to refer to Iran's standard Persian. However, the name *Persian* is still more widely used. The [Academy of Persian Language and Literature](/wiki/Academy_of_Persian_Language_and_Literature "Academy of Persian Language and Literature") has maintained that the [endonym](/wiki/Exonym_and_endonym "Exonym and endonym") **Farsi** is to be avoided in foreign languages, and that *Persian* is the appropriate designation of the language in English, as it has the longer tradition in western languages and better expresses the role of the language as a mark of cultural and national continuity.[[38]](#cite_note-Farhangestan-40) Iranian historian and linguist [Ehsan Yarshater](/wiki/Ehsan_Yarshater "Ehsan Yarshater"), founder of the *[Encyclopædia Iranica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Iranica "Encyclopædia Iranica")* and [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University")'s Center for Iranian Studies, mentions the same concern in an academic journal on [Iranology](/wiki/Iranian_studies "Iranian studies"), rejecting the use of **Farsi** in foreign languages.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

Etymologically, the term *Farsi* derives from its earlier form *Pārsi* (*Pārsik* in [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian")), which in turn comes from the same root as the English term *Persian*.[[40]](#cite_note-Spooner-42) In the same process, the Middle Persian toponym *Pārs* ("Persia") evolved into the modern name Fars.[[41]](#cite_note-43) The phonemic shift from /p/ to /f/ is due to the [influence of Arabic](/wiki/Influence_of_Arabic_on_other_languages "Influence of Arabic on other languages") in the Middle Ages, from the lack of the phoneme /p/ in Standard Arabic.[[42]](#cite_note-44)[[43]](#cite_note-45)[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47)

### Standard varieties' names

The standard Persian of Iran has been called, apart from *Persian* and *Farsi*, by names such as *Iranian Persian* and *Western Persian*, exclusively.[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) The official language of Iran is designated simply as *Persian* (فارسی, *fārsi*).[[10]](#cite_note-Iran_Constitution-10)

The standard Persian of Afghanistan has been officially named *Dari* (دری, *dari*) since 1958.[[15]](#cite_note-Olesen-17) Also referred to as *Afghan Persian* in English, it is one of Afghanistan's two official languages, together with [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language"). The term *Dari*, meaning "of the court", originally referred to the variety of Persian used in the court of the Sasanian Empire in capital [Ctesiphon](/wiki/Ctesiphon "Ctesiphon"), which spread to the northeast of the empire and gradually replaced the former Iranian dialects of [Parthia](/wiki/Parthia "Parthia") ([Parthian](/wiki/Parthian_language "Parthian language")).[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51)

Tajik Persian (форси́и тоҷикӣ́, *forsi-i tojikī*), the standard Persian of Tajikistan, has been officially designated as *Tajik* (тоҷикӣ, **tojikī**) since the time of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[17]](#cite_note-Baker-19) It is the name given to the varieties of Persian spoken in Central Asia in general.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

### ISO codes

The international language-encoding standard [ISO 639-1](/wiki/ISO_639-1 "ISO 639-1") uses the code `fa` for the Persian language, as its coding system is mostly based on the native-language designations. The more detailed standard [ISO 639-3](/wiki/ISO_639-3 "ISO 639-3") uses the code `fas` for the dialects spoken across Iran and Afghanistan.[[51]](#cite_note-53) This consists of the individual languages Dari (`prs`) and Iranian Persian (`pes`). It uses `tgk` for Tajik, separately.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

## History

In general, the Iranian languages are known from three periods: namely Old, Middle, and New (Modern). These correspond to three historical eras of [Iranian history](/wiki/History_of_Iran "History of Iran"); Old era being sometime around the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") (i.e., 400–300 BC), Middle era being the next period most officially around the [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), and New era being the period afterward down to present day.[[53]](#cite_note-EI-IL-vi-55)

According to available documents, the Persian language is "the only Iranian language"[[20]](#cite_note-Lazard-22) for which close philological relationships between all of its three stages are established and so that Old, Middle, and New Persian represent[[20]](#cite_note-Lazard-22)[[54]](#cite_note-EI-IL-vi(2)-56) one and the same language of Persian; that is, New Persian is a direct descendant of Middle and Old Persian.[[54]](#cite_note-EI-IL-vi(2)-56) Gernot Windfuhr considers new Persian as an evolution of the Old Persian language and the Middle Persian language[[55]](#cite_note-57) but also states that none of the known Middle Persian dialects is the direct predecessor of Modern Persian.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59) Ludwig Paul states: "The language of the Shahnameh should be seen as one instance of continuous historical development from Middle to New Persian."[[58]](#cite_note-60)

The known history of the Persian language can be divided into the following three distinct periods:

### Old Persian

Main article: [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian "Old Persian")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Pers%C3%A9polis._Inscription.jpg/250px-Pers%C3%A9polis._Inscription.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pers%C3%A9polis._Inscription.jpg)

An [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian "Old Persian") inscription written in [Old Persian cuneiform](/wiki/Old_Persian_cuneiform "Old Persian cuneiform") in [Persepolis](/wiki/Persepolis "Persepolis"), Iran

As a [written language](/wiki/Written_language "Written language"), Old Persian is attested in royal [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") inscriptions. The oldest known text written in Old Persian is from the [Behistun Inscription](/wiki/Behistun_Inscription "Behistun Inscription"), dating to the time of King [Darius I](/wiki/Darius_I "Darius I") (reigned 522–486 BC).[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmitt200880–1-61)[*[citation not found](/wiki/Template:Harvard_citation_documentation#Wikilink_to_citation_does_not_work "Template:Harvard citation documentation")*] Examples of Old Persian have been found in what is now [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), Romania ([Gherla](/wiki/Gherla "Gherla")),[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhrt2013197-62)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrye1984103-63)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmitt200053-64) [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia"), [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain"), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), Turkey, and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt").[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-OPGTL_6-66) Old Persian is one of the earliest attested Indo-European languages.[[65]](#cite_note-EI-IL-vi-vi(2)-67)

According to certain historical assumptions about the early history and origin of ancient Persians in [Southwestern Iran](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province") (where Achaemenids hailed from), Old Persian was originally spoken by a tribe called *Parsuwash*, who arrived in the [Iranian Plateau](/wiki/Iranian_Plateau "Iranian Plateau") early in the 1st millennium BCE and finally migrated down into the area of present-day Fārs province. Their language, Old Persian, became the official language of the Achaemenid kings.[[65]](#cite_note-EI-IL-vi-vi(2)-67) Assyrian records, which in fact appear to provide the earliest evidence for ancient Iranian (Persian and Median) presence on the Iranian Plateau, give a good chronology but only an approximate geographical indication of what seem to be ancient Persians. In these records of the 9th century BCE, *Parsuwash* (along with *Matai*, presumably Medians) are first mentioned in the area of [Lake Urmia](/wiki/Lake_Urmia "Lake Urmia") in the records of [Shalmaneser III](/wiki/Shalmaneser_III "Shalmaneser III").[[66]](#cite_note-EI-IL-vi-vi(1)-68) The exact identity of the Parsuwash is not known for certain, but from a linguistic viewpoint the word matches Old Persian *pārsa* itself coming directly from the older word \**pārćwa*.[[66]](#cite_note-EI-IL-vi-vi(1)-68) Also, as Old Persian contains many words from another extinct Iranian language, [Median](/wiki/Median_language "Median language"), according to [P. O. Skjærvø](/wiki/Prods_Oktor_Skjaervo "Prods Oktor Skjaervo") it is probable that Old Persian had already been spoken before the formation of the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") and was spoken during most of the first half of the first millennium BCE.[[65]](#cite_note-EI-IL-vi-vi(2)-67) [Xenophon](/wiki/Xenophon "Xenophon"), a Greek general serving in some of the Persian expeditions, describes many aspects of Armenian village life and hospitality in around 401 BCE, which is when Old Persian was still spoken and extensively used. He relates that the [Armenian people](/wiki/Armenian_people "Armenian people") spoke a [language](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language") that to his ear sounded like the language of the Persians.[[67]](#cite_note-69)

Related to Old Persian, but from a different branch of the Iranian language family, was [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan"), the language of the [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism") liturgical texts.

### Middle Persian

Main article: [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Stone_block_with_Paikuli_inscription.JPG/250px-Stone_block_with_Paikuli_inscription.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stone_block_with_Paikuli_inscription.JPG)

[Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian") text written in [Inscriptional Pahlavi](/wiki/Inscriptional_Pahlavi "Inscriptional Pahlavi") on the [Paikuli inscription](/wiki/Paikuli_inscription "Paikuli inscription") from between 293 and 297. [Slemani Museum](/wiki/Sulaymaniyah_Museum "Sulaymaniyah Museum"), [Iraqi Kurdistan](/wiki/Iraqi_Kurdistan "Iraqi Kurdistan").

The complex [grammatical conjugation](/wiki/Grammatical_conjugation "Grammatical conjugation") and [declension](/wiki/Declension "Declension") of Old Persian yielded to the structure of Middle Persian in which the dual number disappeared, leaving only singular and plural, as did gender. Middle Persian developed the [ezāfe](/wiki/Ez%C4%81fe "Ezāfe") construction, expressed through *ī* (modern *e/ye*), to indicate some of the relations between words that have been lost with the simplification of the earlier grammatical system.

Although the "middle period" of the Iranian languages formally begins with the fall of the Achaemenid Empire, the transition from Old to Middle Persian had probably already begun before the 4th century BC. However, Middle Persian is not actually attested until 600 years later when it appears in the Sassanid era (224–651 AD) inscriptions, so any form of the language before this date cannot be described with any degree of certainty. Moreover, as a literary language, Middle Persian is not attested until much later, in the 6th or 7th century. From the 8th century onward, Middle Persian gradually began yielding to New Persian, with the middle-period form only continuing in the texts of [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism").

Middle Persian is considered to be a later form of the same dialect as Old Persian.[[68]](#cite_note-70) The [native name](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") of Middle Persian was *Parsig* or *Parsik*, after the name of the ethnic group of the southwest, that is, "of *Pars*", Old Persian *Parsa*, New Persian *[Fars](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province")*. This is the origin of the name *Farsi* as it is today used to signify New Persian. Following the collapse of the Sassanid state, *Parsik* came to be applied exclusively to (either Middle or New) Persian that was written in the [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script"). From about the 9th century onward, as Middle Persian was on the threshold of becoming New Persian, the older form of the language came to be erroneously called *[Pahlavi](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian")*, which was actually but one of the *writing systems* used to render both Middle Persian as well as various other Middle Iranian languages. That writing system had previously been adopted by the Sassanids (who were Persians, i.e. from the southwest) from the preceding Arsacids (who were Parthians, i.e. from the northeast). While [Ibn al-Muqaffa'](/wiki/Ibn_al-Muqaffa%27 "Ibn al-Muqaffa'") (eighth century) still distinguished between *Pahlavi* (i.e. Parthian) and *Persian* (in Arabic text: al-Farisiyah) (i.e. Middle Persian), this distinction is not evident in Arab commentaries written after that date.

### New Persian

Main article: [New Persian](/wiki/New_Persian "New Persian")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Rudaba.JPG/250px-Rudaba.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rudaba.JPG)

[Ferdowsi](/wiki/Ferdowsi "Ferdowsi")'s *[Shahnameh](/wiki/Shahnameh "Shahnameh")*

"New Persian" (also referred to as Modern Persian) is conventionally divided into three stages:

* Early New Persian (8th/9th centuries)
* Classical Persian (10th–18th centuries)
* Contemporary Persian (19th century to present)

Early New Persian remains largely intelligible to speakers of Contemporary Persian, as the morphology and, to a lesser extent, the lexicon of the language have remained relatively stable.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

#### Early New Persian

New Persian texts written in the [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script") first appear in the 9th-century.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaul2000-72) The language is a direct descendant of Middle Persian, the official, religious, and literary language of the Sasanian Empire (224–651).[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELazard1975596-73) However, it is not descended from the literary form of Middle Persian (known as *pārsīg*, commonly called Pahlavi), which was spoken by the people of [Fars](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province") and used in [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrian "Zoroastrian") religious writings. Instead, it is descended from the dialect spoken by the court of the Sasanian capital [Ctesiphon](/wiki/Ctesiphon "Ctesiphon") and the northeastern Iranian region of [Khorasan](/wiki/Greater_Khorasan "Greater Khorasan"), known as Dari.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaul2000-72)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry2011-74) The region, which comprised the present territories of northwestern Afghanistan as well as parts of Central Asia, played a leading role in the rise of New Persian. Khorasan, which was the homeland of the Parthians, was Persianized under the Sasanians. Dari Persian thus supplanted [Parthian language](/wiki/Parthian_language "Parthian language") (*pahlavānīg*), which by the end of the Sasanian era had fallen out of use.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaul2000-72) New Persian has incorporated many foreign words, including from [eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages") northern and northern Iranian languages such as [Sogdian](/wiki/Sogdian_language "Sogdian language") and especially Parthian.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELazard1975597-75)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Persian_notes_on_Quranic_booklets%2C_written_by_a_native_of_T%C5%ABs_called_Ahmad_Khayq%C4%81n%C4%AB_%28905_CE%29.jpg/250px-Persian_notes_on_Quranic_booklets%2C_written_by_a_native_of_T%C5%ABs_called_Ahmad_Khayq%C4%81n%C4%AB_%28905_CE%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Persian_notes_on_Quranic_booklets,_written_by_a_native_of_T%C5%ABs_called_Ahmad_Khayq%C4%81n%C4%AB_(905_CE).jpg)

Persian notes on [Quranic](/wiki/Quran "Quran") booklets, written by a native of [Tus](/wiki/Tus,_Iran "Tus, Iran") called Ahmad Khayqani in 292 AH (905 CE).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/%DA%A9%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%A8_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%86%DB%8C%D9%87_%D8%B9%D9%86_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AD%D9%82%D8%A7%DB%8C%D9%82_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%AF%D9%88%DB%8C%D9%87%D8%8C_%D8%A8%D8%B1%DA%AF%DB%8C_%D8%A7%D8%B2_%D8%AF%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%86%D9%88%DB%8C%D8%B3_%D9%85%D8%AD%D9%81%D9%88%D8%B8_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%DA%A9%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%A8%D8%AE%D8%A7%D9%86%D9%87_%D9%85%D9%84%DB%8C_%D8%A7%D8%AA%D8%B1%DB%8C%D8%B4_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%D9%88%DB%8C%D9%86.jpg/250px-%DA%A9%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%A8_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%86%DB%8C%D9%87_%D8%B9%D9%86_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AD%D9%82%D8%A7%DB%8C%D9%82_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%AF%D9%88%DB%8C%D9%87%D8%8C_%D8%A8%D8%B1%DA%AF%DB%8C_%D8%A7%D8%B2_%D8%AF%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%86%D9%88%DB%8C%D8%B3_%D9%85%D8%AD%D9%81%D9%88%D8%B8_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%DA%A9%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%A8%D8%AE%D8%A7%D9%86%D9%87_%D9%85%D9%84%DB%8C_%D8%A7%D8%AA%D8%B1%DB%8C%D8%B4_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%D9%88%DB%8C%D9%86.jpg)](/wiki/File:%DA%A9%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%A8_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%A8%D9%86%DB%8C%D9%87_%D8%B9%D9%86_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AD%D9%82%D8%A7%DB%8C%D9%82_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A7%D8%AF%D9%88%DB%8C%D9%87%D8%8C_%D8%A8%D8%B1%DA%AF%DB%8C_%D8%A7%D8%B2_%D8%AF%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%86%D9%88%DB%8C%D8%B3_%D9%85%D8%AD%D9%81%D9%88%D8%B8_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%DA%A9%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%A8%D8%AE%D8%A7%D9%86%D9%87_%D9%85%D9%84%DB%8C_%D8%A7%D8%AA%D8%B1%DB%8C%D8%B4_%D8%AF%D8%B1_%D9%88%DB%8C%D9%86.jpg)

A page from a manuscript of "Kitab al-Abniya 'an Haqa'iq al-Adwiya" by [Abu Mansur Muwaffaq](/wiki/Abu_Mansur_Muwaffaq "Abu Mansur Muwaffaq"), Copied by [Asadi Tusi](/wiki/Asadi_Tusi "Asadi Tusi") in 447 AH (1055 CE).

The transition to New Persian was already complete by the era of the three princely dynasties of Iranian origin, the [Tahirid dynasty](/wiki/Tahirid_dynasty "Tahirid dynasty") (820–872), [Saffarid dynasty](/wiki/Saffarid_dynasty "Saffarid dynasty") (860–903), and [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire") (874–999).[[74]](#cite_note-Public_Domain-76) Abbas of [Merv](/wiki/Merv "Merv") is mentioned as being the earliest minstrel to chant verse in the New Persian tongue and after him the poems of [Hanzala Badghisi](/wiki/Hanzala_Badghisi "Hanzala Badghisi") were among the most famous between the Persian-speakers of the time.[[75]](#cite_note-77)

The first significant Persian poet was [Rudaki](/wiki/Rudaki "Rudaki"). He flourished in the 10th century, when the Samanids were at the height of their power. His reputation as a court poet and as an accomplished musician and singer has survived, although little of his poetry has been preserved. Among his lost works are versified fables collected in the *[Kalila wa Dimna](/wiki/Kal%C4%ABla_wa-Dimna "Kalīla wa-Dimna")*.[[23]](#cite_note-Persian_literature-25)

The language spread geographically from the 11th century on and was the medium through which, among others, Central Asian Turks became familiar with Islam and urban culture. New Persian was widely used as a trans-regional [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca"), a task aided due to its relatively simple morphology, and this situation persisted until at least the 19th century.[[76]](#cite_note-turkiclanguages.com-78) In the late Middle Ages, new Islamic literary languages were created on the Persian model: [Ottoman Turkish](/wiki/Ottoman_Turkish_language "Ottoman Turkish language"), [Chagatai Turkic](/wiki/Chagatai_language "Chagatai language"), [Dobhashi Bengali](/wiki/Dobhashi "Dobhashi"), and Urdu, which are regarded as "structural daughter languages" of Persian.[[76]](#cite_note-turkiclanguages.com-78)

#### Classical Persian

See also: [List of Persian-language poets and authors](/wiki/List_of_Persian-language_poets_and_authors "List of Persian-language poets and authors")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Kalila_wa_Dimna_001.jpg/250px-Kalila_wa_Dimna_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kalila_wa_Dimna_001.jpg)

[*Kalilah va Dimna*](/wiki/Panchatantra "Panchatantra"), an influential work in Persian literature

"Classical Persian" loosely refers to the standardized language of [medieval Persia](/wiki/Medieval_Persia "Medieval Persia") used in [literature](/wiki/Persian_literature "Persian literature") and [poetry](/wiki/Persian_poetry "Persian poetry").
This is the language of the 10th to 12th centuries, which continued to be used as literary language and [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") under the "[Persianized](/wiki/Persianized "Persianized")" Turko-Mongol dynasties during the 12th to 15th centuries, and under restored Persian rule during the 16th to 19th centuries.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

Persian during this time served as lingua franca of [Greater Persia](/wiki/Greater_Persia "Greater Persia") and of much of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").
It was also the official and cultural language of many Islamic dynasties, including the Samanids, [Buyids](/wiki/Buyid_dynasty "Buyid dynasty"), [Tahirids](/wiki/Tahirid_dynasty "Tahirid dynasty"), [Ziyarids](/wiki/Ziyarid_dynasty "Ziyarid dynasty"), the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), [Timurids](/wiki/Timurid_Empire "Timurid Empire"), [Ghaznavids](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids"), [Karakhanids](/wiki/Kara-Khanid_Khanate "Kara-Khanid Khanate"), [Seljuqs](/wiki/Seljuq_dynasty "Seljuq dynasty"), [Khwarazmians](/wiki/Khwarazmian_dynasty "Khwarazmian dynasty"), the [Sultanate of Rum](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Rum "Sultanate of Rum"), [Turkmen beyliks of Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolian_beyliks "Anatolian beyliks"), [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"), the [Shirvanshahs](/wiki/Shirvanshah "Shirvanshah"), [Safavids](/wiki/Safavid_dynasty "Safavid dynasty"), [Afsharids](/wiki/Afsharid_dynasty "Afsharid dynasty"), [Zands](/wiki/Zand_dynasty "Zand dynasty"), [Qajars](/wiki/Qajar_dynasty "Qajar dynasty"), [Khanate of Bukhara](/wiki/Khanate_of_Bukhara "Khanate of Bukhara"), [Khanate of Kokand](/wiki/Khanate_of_Kokand "Khanate of Kokand"), [Emirate of Bukhara](/wiki/Emirate_of_Bukhara "Emirate of Bukhara"), [Khanate of Khiva](/wiki/Khanate_of_Khiva "Khanate of Khiva"), [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), and also many Mughal successors such as the [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad").
Persian was the only non-European language known and used by [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo") at the court of [Kublai Khan](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan") and in his journeys through China.[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81)

##### Use in Asia Minor

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Ottoman_miniature_painters.jpg/250px-Ottoman_miniature_painters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ottoman_miniature_painters.jpg)

Persian on an [Ottoman miniature](/wiki/Ottoman_miniature "Ottoman miniature")

A branch of the Seljuks, the [Sultanate of Rum](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Rum "Sultanate of Rum"), took Persian language, art, and letters to Anatolia.[[80]](#cite_note-Sigfried_J_1994._p_734-82) They adopted the Persian language as the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the empire.[[81]](#cite_note-83) The [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), who can roughly be seen as their eventual successors, inherited this tradition. Persian was the official court language of the empire, and for some time, the official language of the empire.[[82]](#cite_note-84) The educated and noble class of the Ottoman Empire all spoke Persian, such as Sultan [Selim I](/wiki/Selim_I "Selim I"), despite being Safavid Iran's archrival and a staunch opposer of [Shia Islam](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpuler200368-85) It was a major literary language in the empire.[[84]](#cite_note-86) Some of the noted earlier Persian works during the Ottoman rule are [Idris Bidlisi](/wiki/Idris_Bitlisi "Idris Bitlisi")'s *Hasht Bihisht*, which began in 1502 and covered the reign of the first eight Ottoman rulers, and the *Salim-Namah*, a glorification of Selim I.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpuler200368-85) After a period of several centuries, [Ottoman Turkish](/wiki/Ottoman_Turkish_language "Ottoman Turkish language") (which was highly Persianised itself) had developed toward a fully accepted language of literature, and which was even able to lexically satisfy the demands of a scientific presentation.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpuler200369-87) However, the number of Persian and Arabic loanwords contained in those works increased at times up to 88%.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpuler200369-87) In the Ottoman Empire, Persian was used at the royal court, for diplomacy, poetry, historiographical works, literary works, and was taught in state schools, and was also offered as an elective course or recommended for study in some *[madrasas](/wiki/Madrasa "Madrasa")*.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

##### Use in the Balkans

Persian learning was also widespread in the Ottoman-held [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") (*[Rumelia](/wiki/Rumelia "Rumelia")*), with a range of cities being famed for their long-standing traditions in the study of Persian and its classics, amongst them Saraybosna (modern [Sarajevo](/wiki/Sarajevo "Sarajevo"), Bosnia and Herzegovina), [Mostar](/wiki/Mostar "Mostar") (also in Bosnia and Herzegovina), and Vardar Yenicesi (or Yenice-i Vardar, now [Giannitsa](/wiki/Giannitsa "Giannitsa"), in northern Greece).[[87]](#cite_note-89)

Vardar Yenicesi differed from other localities in the Balkans insofar as that it was a town where Persian was also widely spoken.[[88]](#cite_note-Green-90) However, the Persian of Vardar Yenicesi and of the rest of the Ottoman-held Balkans was different from formal Persian both in accent and vocabulary.[[88]](#cite_note-Green-90) The difference was apparent to such a degree that the Ottomans referred to it as "Rumelian Persian" (*Rumili Farsisi*).[[88]](#cite_note-Green-90) As learned people such as students, scholars and literati often frequented Vardar Yenicesi, it soon became the site of a flourishing [Persianate](/wiki/Persianate_society "Persianate society") linguistic and literary culture.[[88]](#cite_note-Green-90) The 16th-century Ottoman [Aşık Çelebi](/wiki/A%C5%9F%C4%B1k_%C3%87elebi "Aşık Çelebi") (died 1572), who hailed from [Prizren](/wiki/Prizren "Prizren") in modern-day [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), was galvanized by the abundant Persian-speaking and Persian-writing communities of Vardar Yenicesi, and he referred to the city as a "hotbed of Persian".[[88]](#cite_note-Green-90)

Many Ottoman Persianists who established a career in the Ottoman capital of Constantinople (modern-day [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul")) pursued early Persian training in Saraybosna, amongst them [Ahmed Sudi](/wiki/Ahmed_Sudi "Ahmed Sudi").[[89]](#cite_note-91)

##### Use in Indian subcontinent

Main article: [Persian language in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Persian_language_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Persian language in the Indian subcontinent")

See also: [Persian and Urdu](/wiki/Persian_and_Urdu "Persian and Urdu") and [Dobhashi](/wiki/Dobhashi "Dobhashi")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Agra_India_persian_poem.jpg/250px-Agra_India_persian_poem.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agra_India_persian_poem.jpg)

Persian poem, [Agra Fort](/wiki/Agra_Fort "Agra Fort"), India, 18th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Agra_castle_India_persian_poem.jpg/250px-Agra_castle_India_persian_poem.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agra_castle_India_persian_poem.jpg)

Persian poem, *Takht-e Shah Jahan*, [Agra Fort](/wiki/Agra_Fort "Agra Fort"), India

The Persian language influenced the formation of many modern languages in West Asia, Europe, [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). Following the Turko-Persian [Ghaznavid](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni") conquest of [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), Persian was firstly introduced in the region by Turkic Central Asians.[[90]](#cite_note-92) The basis in general for the introduction of Persian language into the subcontinent was set, from its earliest days, by various Persianized Central Asian Turkic and Afghan dynasties.[[80]](#cite_note-Sigfried_J_1994._p_734-82) For five centuries prior to the [British colonization](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), Persian was widely used as a second language in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). It took prominence as the language of culture and education in several Muslim courts on the subcontinent and became the sole "official language" under the [Mughal emperors](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire").

The [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate") witnessed an influx of Persian scholars, lawyers, teachers, and clerics. Thousands of Persian books and manuscripts were published in Bengal. The period of the reign of Sultan [Ghiyathuddin Azam Shah](/wiki/Ghiyathuddin_Azam_Shah "Ghiyathuddin Azam Shah") is described as the "golden age of Persian literature in Bengal". Its stature was illustrated by the Sultan's own correspondence and collaboration with the Persian poet [Hafez](/wiki/Hafez "Hafez"); a poem which can be found in the *Divan of Hafez* today.[[91]](#cite_note-93) A [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") dialect emerged among the common [Bengali Muslim](/wiki/Bengali_Muslim "Bengali Muslim") folk, based on a Persian model and known as [Dobhashi](/wiki/Dobhashi "Dobhashi"); meaning *mixed language*. Dobhashi Bengali was patronised and given official status under the [Sultans of Bengal](/wiki/Sultans_of_Bengal "Sultans of Bengal"), and was a popular literary form used by Bengalis during the pre-colonial period, irrespective of their religion.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

Following the defeat of the [Hindu Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") dynasty, classical Persian was established as a courtly language in the region during the late 10th century under [Ghaznavid](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids") rule over the northwestern frontier of the [subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[93]](#cite_note-google2-95) Employed by [Punjabis](/wiki/Punjabis "Punjabis") in literature, Persian achieved prominence in the region during the following centuries.[[93]](#cite_note-google2-95) Persian continued to act as a courtly language for various empires in [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region") through the early 19th century serving finally as the official state language of the [Sikh Empire](/wiki/Sikh_Empire "Sikh Empire"), preceding [British conquest](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)") and the decline of Persian in South Asia.[[94]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition_1911_Page_892-96)[[95]](#cite_note-Grewal112-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98)

Beginning in 1843, though, English and [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") gradually replaced Persian in importance on the subcontinent.[[97]](#cite_note-99) Evidence of Persian's historical influence there can be seen in the extent of its influence on certain languages of the Indian subcontinent. Words borrowed from Persian are still quite commonly used in certain Indo-Aryan languages, especially [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi")-[Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") (also historically known as [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language")), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language"), and [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language").[[98]](#cite_note-google4-100) There is also a small population of Zoroastrian [Iranis](/wiki/Irani_(India) "Irani (India)") in India, who migrated in the 19th century to escape religious persecution in [Qajar Iran](/wiki/Qajar_dynasty "Qajar dynasty") and speak a Dari dialect.

#### Contemporary Persian

##### Qajar dynasty

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Persische_dialekte_Persian_dialects.png/250px-Persische_dialekte_Persian_dialects.png)](/wiki/File:Persische_dialekte_Persian_dialects.png)

Persian dialects

In the 19th century, under the [Qajar dynasty](/wiki/Qajar_dynasty "Qajar dynasty"), the dialect that is spoken in [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran") rose to prominence. There was still substantial Arabic vocabulary, but many of these words have been integrated into Persian phonology and grammar. In addition, under the Qajar rule, numerous [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and English terms entered the Persian language, especially vocabulary related to technology.

The first official attentions to the necessity of protecting the Persian language against foreign words, and to the standardization of [Persian orthography](/wiki/Persian_alphabet "Persian alphabet"), were under the reign of [Naser ed Din Shah](/wiki/Naser_al-Din_Shah_Qajar "Naser al-Din Shah Qajar") of the [Qajar dynasty](/wiki/Qajar_dynasty "Qajar dynasty") in 1871.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After Naser ed Din Shah, [Mozaffar ed Din Shah](/wiki/Mozaffar_ad-Din_Shah_Qajar "Mozaffar ad-Din Shah Qajar") ordered the establishment of the first Persian association in 1903.[[38]](#cite_note-Farhangestan-40) This association officially declared that it used Persian and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") as acceptable sources for coining words. The ultimate goal was to prevent books from being printed with wrong use of words. According to the executive guarantee of this association, the government was responsible for wrongfully printed books. Words coined by this association, such as *rāh-āhan* (راه‌آهن) for "railway", were printed in *Soltani Newspaper*; but the association was eventually closed due to inattention.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A scientific association was founded in 1911, resulting in a dictionary called *Words of Scientific Association* (لغت انجمن علمی), which was completed later and renamed *Katouzian Dictionary* (فرهنگ کاتوزیان).[[99]](#cite_note-101)

The first academy for the Persian language was founded on 20 May 1935, under the name *Academy of Iran*. It was established by the initiative of [Reza Shah Pahlavi](/wiki/Reza_Shah_Pahlavi "Reza Shah Pahlavi"), and mainly by [Hekmat e Shirazi](/wiki/Hekmat_E_Shirazi "Hekmat E Shirazi") and [Mohammad Ali Foroughi](/wiki/Mohammad_Ali_Foroughi "Mohammad Ali Foroughi"), all prominent names in the nationalist movement of the time.
The academy was a key institution in the struggle to re-build Iran as a nation-state after the collapse of the Qajar dynasty. During the 1930s and 1940s, the academy led massive campaigns to replace the many [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") loanwords whose widespread use in Persian during the centuries preceding the foundation of the Pahlavi dynasty had created a literary language considerably different from the spoken Persian of the time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This became the basis of what is now known as "Contemporary Standard Persian".

## Varieties

There are three standard varieties of modern Persian:

* *[Iranian Persian](/wiki/Iranian_Persian "Iranian Persian")* (*Persian*, *Western Persian*, or *Farsi*) is spoken in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), and by minorities in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") and the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") states.
* *[Eastern Persian](/wiki/Dari "Dari")* (*Dari Persian*, *Afghan Persian*, or *Dari*) is spoken in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan").
* *[Tajiki](/wiki/Tajik_language "Tajik language")* (*Tajik Persian*) is spoken in [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") and [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"). It is written in the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script").

All three varieties are based on the classic Persian literature and its literary tradition. There are also several local dialects from Iran, Afghanistan and Tajikistan which slightly differ from the standard Persian. The [Hazaragi dialect](/wiki/Hazaragi_dialect "Hazaragi dialect") (in Afghanistan), [Herati](/wiki/Herat "Herat") (in Western Afghanistan), Darwazi (in Afghanistan and Tajikistan), [Basseri](/wiki/Basseri_dialect "Basseri dialect") (in Southern Iran), and the [Tehrani accent](/wiki/Tehrani_accent "Tehrani accent") (in Iran, the basis of standard Iranian Persian) are examples of these dialects. Persian-speaking peoples of Iran, Afghanistan, and Tajikistan can understand one another with a relatively high degree of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility").[[100]](#cite_note-102) Nevertheless, the *[Encyclopædia Iranica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Iranica "Encyclopædia Iranica")* notes that the Iranian, Afghan, and Tajiki varieties comprise distinct branches of the Persian language, and within each branch a wide variety of local dialects exist.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

The following are some languages closely related to Persian, or in some cases are considered dialects:

* [Luri](/wiki/Luri_language "Luri language") (or *Lori*), spoken mainly in the southwestern Iranian provinces of [Lorestan](/wiki/Lorestan_province "Lorestan province"), [Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad](/wiki/Kohgiluyeh_and_Boyer-Ahmad_province "Kohgiluyeh and Boyer-Ahmad province"), [Chaharmahal and Bakhtiari](/wiki/Chaharmahal_and_Bakhtiari_province "Chaharmahal and Bakhtiari province") some western parts of [Fars province](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province"), and some parts of [Khuzestan province](/wiki/Khuzestan_province "Khuzestan province").
* [Achomi](/wiki/Achomi_language "Achomi language") (or *Lari*), spoken mainly in southern Iranian provinces of [Fars](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province") and [Hormozgan](/wiki/Hormozgan_province "Hormozgan province"), unlike [New Persian](/wiki/New_Persian "New Persian") and its variants like [Dari](/wiki/Dari "Dari"), [Standard Persian](/wiki/Standard_Persian "Standard Persian"), and [Iranian Persian](/wiki/Iranian_Persian "Iranian Persian"), this is a branch of [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian").[[102]](#cite_note-:62-104)[[103]](#cite_note-:7-105)[[104]](#cite_note-:8-106)
* [Tat](/wiki/Tat_language_(Caucasus) "Tat language (Caucasus)"), spoken in parts of Azerbaijan, Russia, and Transcaucasia. It is classified as a variety of Persian.[[105]](#cite_note-107)[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109)[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-111) (This dialect is not to be confused with the [Tati language](/wiki/Tati_language_(Iran) "Tati language (Iran)") of northwestern Iran, which is a member of a different branch of the Iranian languages.)
* [Judeo-Tat](/wiki/Judeo-Tat "Judeo-Tat"). Part of the Tat-Persian continuum, spoken in Azerbaijan, Russia, as well as by immigrant communities in Israel and New York.

More distantly related branches of the [Iranian language family](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages") include Kurdish and [Balochi](/wiki/Balochi_language "Balochi language").

The [Glottolog](/wiki/Glottolog "Glottolog") database proposes the following phylogenetic classification:

* Farsic–Caucasian Tat
  + Caucasian Tat
    - [Judeo-Tat](/wiki/Judeo-Tat "Judeo-Tat")
    - [Muslim Tat](/wiki/Tat_language_(Caucasus) "Tat language (Caucasus)") (including Armeno-Tat)
  + Farsic
    - Eastern Farsic
      * [Aimaq](/wiki/Aimaq_dialect "Aimaq dialect")
      * [Dari](/wiki/Dari "Dari")
      * [Dehwari](/wiki/Dehwari_language "Dehwari language")
      * [Hazaragi](/wiki/Hazaragi_dialect "Hazaragi dialect")
      * [Pahlavani](/wiki/Pahlavani_language "Pahlavani language")
      * Tajikic
        + [Bukharic](/wiki/Bukharian_(Judeo-Tajik_dialect) "Bukharian (Judeo-Tajik dialect)")
        + [Tajik](/wiki/Tajik_language "Tajik language")
    - [Judeo-Persian](/wiki/Judeo-Persian "Judeo-Persian")
    - [Western Farsi](/wiki/Iranian_Persian "Iranian Persian")

## Phonology

Main article: [Persian phonology](/wiki/Persian_phonology "Persian phonology")

Iranian Persian and Tajik have six vowels; Dari has eight. Iranian Persian has twenty-three consonants, but both Dari and Tajiki have twenty-four consonants, due to the phonemic merger of /q/ and /ɣ/ in Iranian Persian.[[110]](#cite_note-112)

Historically, Persian distinguished length. Early New Persian had a series of five long vowels (/[iː](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel")/, /[uː](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")/, /[ɑː](/wiki/Open_back_unrounded_vowel "Open back unrounded vowel")/, /[oː](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel")/, and /[eː](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel")/) along with three short vowels /[æ](/wiki/Near-open_front_unrounded_vowel "Near-open front unrounded vowel")/, /[i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel")/, and /[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")/. At some point prior to the 16th century in the general area now modern Iran, /eː/ and /iː/ merged into /iː/, and /oː/ and /uː/ merged into /uː/. Thus, older contrasts such as شیر *shēr* "lion" vs. شیر *shīr* "milk", and زود *zūd* "quick" vs زور *zōr* "strength" were lost. However, there are exceptions to this rule, and in some words, *ē* and *ō* are merged into the diphthongs [eɪ] and [oʊ] (which are descendants of the diphthongs [æɪ] and [æʊ] in Early New Persian), instead of merging into /iː/ and /uː/. Examples of the exception can be found in words such as روشن [roʊʃæn] (bright). Numerous other instances exist.

However, in Dari, the archaic distinction of /eː/ and /iː/ (respectively known as یای مجهول *Yā-ye majhūl* and یای معروف *Yā-ye ma'rūf*) is still preserved as well as the distinction of /oː/ and /uː/ (known as واو مجهول *Wāw-e majhūl* and واو معروف *Wāw-e ma'rūf*). On the other hand, in standard Tajik, the length distinction has disappeared, and /iː/ merged with /i/ and /uː/ with /u/.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry2005-113) Therefore, contemporary Afghan Dari dialects are the closest to the vowel inventory of Early New Persian.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOkati201293-114)

According to most studies on the subject, the three vowels traditionally considered long (/i/, /u/, /ɒ/) are currently distinguished from their short counterparts (/e/, /o/, /æ/) by position of articulation rather than by length. However, there are studies that consider vowel length to be the active feature of the system, with /ɒ/, /i/, and /u/ phonologically long or bimoraic and /æ/, /e/, and /o/ phonologically short or monomoraic.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOkati201292-115)

There are also some studies that consider quality and quantity to be both active in the Iranian system. That offers a synthetic analysis including both quality and quantity, which often suggests that Modern Persian vowels are in a transition state between the quantitative system of Classical Persian and a hypothetical future Iranian language, which will eliminate all traces of quantity and retain quality as the only active feature. The length distinction is still strictly observed by careful reciters of classic-style poetry.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOkati201292-115)

## Grammar

Main article: [Persian grammar](/wiki/Persian_grammar "Persian grammar")

### Morphology

Suffixes predominate Persian [morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)"), though there are a small number of prefixes.[[117]](#cite_note-megerdoomian2000-119) Verbs can express tense and [aspect](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect"), and they agree with the subject in person and number.[[118]](#cite_note-mahootian1997-120) While Old Persian had [grammatical gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender")[[119]](#cite_note-121)[[120]](#cite_note-122) there is no [grammatical gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") in modern Persian, and pronouns are not marked for [natural gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender#Grammatical_vs._natural_gender "Grammatical gender"). In other words, in Persian, pronouns are gender-neutral. When referring to a masculine or a feminine subject, the same pronoun [او](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D8%A7%D9%88#Persian "wikt:او") is used (pronounced "ou", ū).[[121]](#cite_note-123)

### Syntax

Persian adheres mainly to subject–object–verb ([SOV](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb_word_order "Subject–object–verb word order")) word order. But case endings (e.g. for subject, object, etc.) expressed via suffixes may allow users to vary word order. Verbs agree with the subject in person and number. Normal declarative sentences are structured as *(S) (PP) (O) V*: sentences have optional [subjects](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)"), [prepositional phrases](/wiki/Prepositional_phrase "Prepositional phrase"), and [objects](/wiki/Object_(grammar) "Object (grammar)") followed by a compulsory [verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb"). If the object is specific, the object is followed by the word *rā* and precedes prepositional phrases: *(S) (O +* rā*) (PP) V*.[[118]](#cite_note-mahootian1997-120)

## Vocabulary

Main article: [Persian vocabulary](/wiki/Persian_vocabulary "Persian vocabulary")

### Native word formation

Persian makes extensive use of word building and combining affixes, stems, nouns, and adjectives. Persian frequently uses derivational [agglutination](/wiki/Agglutination "Agglutination") to [form new words](/wiki/Word_formation "Word formation") from nouns, adjectives, and verbal stems. New words are extensively formed by [compounding](/wiki/Compound_(linguistics) "Compound (linguistics)") – two existing words combining into a new one.

### Influences

See also: [List of English words of Persian origin](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Persian_origin "List of English words of Persian origin"), [List of French loanwords in Persian](/wiki/List_of_French_loanwords_in_Persian "List of French loanwords in Persian"), and [Iranian languages § Comparison table](/wiki/Iranian_languages#Comparison_table "Iranian languages")

While having a lesser influence from [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic")[[28]](#cite_note-Nushin_Namazi-30) and other languages of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") and its core vocabulary being of [Middle Persian](/wiki/Middle_Persian "Middle Persian") origin,[[122]](#cite_note-Richard_Davis_2006._pp._602-603-124) New Persian contains a considerable number of Arabic lexical items,[[20]](#cite_note-Lazard-22)[[27]](#cite_note-Lazard,_Gilbert_1971-29)[[29]](#cite_note-Classe_2000_1057-31) which were Persianized[[30]](#cite_note-lambtonexcerpt-32) and often took a different meaning and usage than the [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") original. Persian loanwords of Arabic origin especially include [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") terms. The Arabic vocabulary in other Iranian, Turkic, and Indic languages is generally understood to have been copied from New Persian, not from Arabic itself.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

[John R. Perry](/wiki/John_R._Perry_(orientalist) "John R. Perry (orientalist)"), in his article "Lexical Areas and Semantic Fields of Arabic", estimates that about 20 percent of everyday vocabulary in current Persian, and around 25 percent of the vocabulary of classical and modern Persian literature, are of Arabic origin. The text frequency of these loan words is generally lower and varies by style and topic area. It may approach 25 percent of a text in literature.[[124]](#cite_note-Perry-126) According to another source, about 40% of everyday Persian literary vocabulary is of Arabic origin.[[125]](#cite_note-127) Among the Arabic loan words, relatively few (14 percent) are from the semantic domain of material culture, while a larger number are from domains of intellectual and spiritual life.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry200599-128) Most of the Arabic words used in Persian are either synonyms of native terms or could be glossed in Persian.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry200599-128)

The inclusion of [Mongolic](/wiki/Mongolic_languages "Mongolic languages") and [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages") elements in the Persian language should also be mentioned,[[127]](#cite_note-129) not only because of the political role a succession of Turkic dynasties played in Iranian history, but also because of the immense prestige Persian language and literature enjoyed in the wider (non-Arab) Islamic world, which was often ruled by sultans and emirs with a Turkic background. The Turkish and Mongolian vocabulary in Persian is minor in comparison to that of Arabic and these words were mainly confined to military, pastoral terms and political sector (titles, administration, etc.).[[128]](#cite_note-130) New military and political titles were coined based partially on Middle Persian (e.g. ارتش *arteš* for "army", instead of the Uzbek قؤشین *qoʻshin*; سرلشکر *sarlaškar*; دریابان *daryābān*; etc.) in the 20th century. Persian has likewise influenced the vocabularies of other languages, especially other [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") such as [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"),[[129]](#cite_note-131) Urdu, Bengali, and Hindi; the latter three through conquests of Persianized Central Asian Turkic and Afghan invaders;[[130]](#cite_note-132) [Turkic languages](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages") such as [Ottoman Turkish](/wiki/Ottoman_Turkish_language "Ottoman Turkish language"), [Chagatai](/wiki/Chagatai_language "Chagatai language"), [Tatar](/wiki/Tatar_language "Tatar language"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"),[[131]](#cite_note-133) [Turkmen](/wiki/Turkmen_language "Turkmen language"), [Azeri](/wiki/Azerbaijani_language "Azerbaijani language"),[[132]](#cite_note-134) [Uzbek](/wiki/Uzbek_language "Uzbek language"), and [Karachay-Balkar](/wiki/Karachay-Balkar_language "Karachay-Balkar language");[[133]](#cite_note-Compendium_of_the_World_Languages-135) [Caucasian languages](/wiki/Languages_of_the_Caucasus "Languages of the Caucasus") such as [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language"),[[134]](#cite_note-136) and, to a lesser extent, [Avar](/wiki/Avar_language "Avar language") and [Lezgin](/wiki/Lezgin_language "Lezgin language");[[135]](#cite_note-137) Afro-Asiatic languages like [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_Neo-Aramaic "Assyrian Neo-Aramaic") ([List of loanwords in Assyrian Neo-Aramaic](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Assyrian_Neo-Aramaic "List of loanwords in Assyrian Neo-Aramaic")) and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), particularly [Bahrani Arabic](/wiki/Bahrani_Arabic "Bahrani Arabic");[[26]](#cite_note-Holes2001-28)[[136]](#cite_note-138) and even [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") indirectly especially [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), and [Brahui](/wiki/Brahui_language "Brahui language"); as well as [Austronesian languages](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages") such as [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") and [Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysian_language "Malaysian language") [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"). Persian has also had a significant lexical influence, via Turkish, on [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language") and [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian "Serbo-Croatian"), particularly as spoken in [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina").

Use of occasional foreign synonyms instead of Persian words can be a common practice in everyday communications as an alternative expression. In some instances in addition to the Persian vocabulary, the equivalent synonyms from multiple foreign languages can be used. For example, in Iranian colloquial Persian (not in Afghanistan or Tajikistan), the phrase "thank you" may be expressed using the French word مرسی *merci* (stressed, however, on the first syllable), the hybrid Persian-Arabic phrase متشکّرَم *motešakkeram* (متشکّر *motešakker* being "thankful" in Arabic, commonly pronounced *moččakker* in Persian, and the verb ـَم *am* meaning "I am" in Persian), or by the pure Persian phrase سپاسگزارم *sepās-gozāram*.

The vast majority of modern Iranian Persian and Dari text is written with the [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script"). Tajiki, which is considered by some linguists to be a Persian dialect influenced by [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") and the [Turkic languages](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages") of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"),[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry2005-113)[[138]](#cite_note-lazard1956-140) is written with the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") in [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") (see [Tajik alphabet](/wiki/Tajik_alphabet "Tajik alphabet")). There also exist several [romanization systems for Persian](/wiki/Romanization_of_Persian "Romanization of Persian").

### Persian alphabet

Main article: [Persian alphabet](/wiki/Persian_alphabet "Persian alphabet")

Modern Iranian Persian and Afghan Persian are written using the [Persian alphabet](/wiki/Persian_alphabet "Persian alphabet"), which is a modified variant of the [Arabic alphabet](/wiki/Arabic_alphabet "Arabic alphabet"), using different pronunciation and additional letters not found in the Arabic language. After the [Arab conquest of Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia"), it took approximately 200 years before Persians adopted the Arabic script in place of the older alphabet. Previously, two different scripts were used, [Pahlavi](/wiki/Pahlavi_scripts "Pahlavi scripts"), used for Middle Persian, and the [Avestan alphabet](/wiki/Avestan_alphabet "Avestan alphabet") (in Persian, Dīndapirak, or Din Dabire—literally: religion script), used for religious purposes, primarily for the [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") but sometimes for Middle Persian.

In the modern Persian script, [historically short vowels](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length") are usually not written, only the historically long ones are represented in the text, so words distinguished from each other only by short vowels are ambiguous in writing: Iranian Persian **kerm** "worm", **karam** "generosity", **kerem** "cream", and **krom** "chrome" are all spelled **krm** (کرم) in Persian. The reader must determine the word from context. The Arabic system of vocalization marks known as *[harakat](/wiki/Harakat "Harakat")* is also used in Persian, although some of the symbols have different pronunciations. For example, a *[ḍammah](/wiki/%E1%B8%8Cammah "Ḍammah")* is pronounced [ʊ~u], while in Iranian Persian it is pronounced [o]. This system is not used in mainstream Persian literature; it is primarily used for teaching and in some (but not all) dictionaries.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Persian_typewriter_keyboard_layout.svg/250px-Persian_typewriter_keyboard_layout.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Persian_typewriter_keyboard_layout.svg)

Persian typewriter keyboard layout

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Persian_keyboard_layout%2C_unshifted.gif/250px-Persian_keyboard_layout%2C_unshifted.gif)](/wiki/File:Persian_keyboard_layout,_unshifted.gif)

A variant of the Iranian standard ISIRI 9147 keyboard layout for Persian

There are several letters generally only used in Arabic loanwords. These letters are pronounced the same as similar Persian letters. For example, there are four functionally identical letters for /[z](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_fricative "Voiced alveolar fricative")/ (ز ذ ض ظ), three letters for /[s](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_fricative "Voiceless alveolar fricative")/ (س ص ث), two letters for /[t](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_plosive "Voiceless alveolar plosive")/ (ط ت), two letters for /[h](/wiki/Voiceless_glottal_fricative "Voiceless glottal fricative")/ (ح ه).
On the other hand, there are four letters that do not exist in Arabic پ چ ژ گ.

#### Additions

The [Persian alphabet](/wiki/Persian_alphabet "Persian alphabet") adds four letters to the Arabic alphabet:

| Sound | Isolated form | Final form | Medial form | Initial form | Name |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| /p/ | پ | ـپ | ـپـ | پـ | *pe* |
| /tʃ/ | چ | ـچ | ـچـ | چـ | *če (che)* |
| /ʒ/ | ژ | ـژ | ـژ | ژ | *že (zhe or jhe)* |
| /ɡ/ | گ | ـگ | ـگـ | گـ | *ge (gāf)* |

Historically, there was also a special letter for the sound /β/. This letter is no longer used, as the /β/-sound changed to /b/, e.g. archaic زڤان /zaβaːn/ > زبان /zæbɒn/ 'language'[[139]](#cite_note-141)

| Sound | Isolated form | Final form | Medial form | Initial form | Name |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| /β/ | ڤ | ـڤ | ـڤـ | ڤـ | *βe* |

#### Variations

The Persian alphabet also modifies some letters of the Arabic alphabet. For example, *alef with hamza below* ( إ ) changes to *[alef](/wiki/Aleph "Aleph")* ( ا ); words using various [hamzas](/wiki/Hamza "Hamza") get spelled with yet another kind of hamza (so that مسؤول becomes مسئول) even though the latter has been accepted in Arabic since the 1980s; and *[teh marbuta](/wiki/Teh_marbuta "Teh marbuta")* ( ة ) changes to *[heh](/wiki/He_(letter) "He (letter)")* ( ه ) or *[teh](/wiki/Taw_(letter) "Taw (letter)")* ( ت ).

The letters different in shape are:

| Arabic style letter | Persian style letter | Name |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ك | ک | *ke* (kāf) |
| ي | ی | *ye* |

However, ی in shape and form is the traditional Arabic style that continues in the Nile Valley, namely, [Egypt](/wiki/Egyptian_Arabic "Egyptian Arabic"), [Sudan](/wiki/Sudanese_Arabic "Sudanese Arabic"), and [South Sudan](/wiki/Juba_Arabic "Juba Arabic").

### Latin alphabet

Main article: [Romanization of Persian](/wiki/Romanization_of_Persian "Romanization of Persian")

The [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") has published a standard for simplified [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") of Persian into Latin, ISO 233-3, titled "Information and documentation – Transliteration of Arabic characters into Latin characters – Part 3: Persian language – Simplified transliteration"[[140]](#cite_note-142) but the transliteration scheme is not in widespread use.

Another Latin alphabet, based on the [New Turkic Alphabet](/wiki/Ya%C3%B1alif "Yañalif"), was used in [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") in the 1920s and 1930s. The alphabet was phased out in favor of [Cyrillic](/wiki/Tajik_Cyrillic_alphabet "Tajik Cyrillic alphabet") in the late 1930s.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry2005-113)

[Fingilish](/wiki/Fingilish "Fingilish") is Persian using [ISO basic Latin alphabet](/wiki/ISO_basic_Latin_alphabet "ISO basic Latin alphabet"). It is most commonly used in [chat](/wiki/Online_chat "Online chat"), [emails](/wiki/Email "Email"), and [SMS](/wiki/Short_Message_Service "Short Message Service") applications. The orthography is not standardized, and varies among writers and even media (for example, typing 'aa' for the [ɒ] phoneme is easier on computer keyboards than on cellphone keyboards, resulting in smaller usage of the combination on cellphones).

### Tajik alphabet

Main article: [Tajik alphabet](/wiki/Tajik_alphabet "Tajik alphabet")

The Cyrillic script was introduced for writing the [Tajik language](/wiki/Tajik_language "Tajik language") under the [Tajik Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Tajik_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Tajik Soviet Socialist Republic") in the late 1930s, replacing the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Tajik_alphabet#Transliteration_standards "Tajik alphabet") that had been used since the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") and the Persian script that had been used earlier. After 1939, materials published in Persian in the Persian script were banned in the country.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerry2005-113)[[141]](#cite_note-143)

During this period, Persian was adopted as the lingua franca of the eastern Islamic nations. Extensive contact with Arabic led to a large influx of Arab vocabulary. In fact, a writer of Classical Persian had at one's disposal the entire Arabic lexicon and could use Arab terms freely either for literary effect or to display erudition. Classical Persian remained essentially unchanged until the nineteenth century, when the dialect of Teheran rose in prominence, having been chosen as the capital of Persia by the Qajar Dynasty in 1787. This Modern Persian dialect became the basis of what is now called Contemporary Standard Persian. Although it still contains a large number of Arab terms, most borrowings have been nativized, with a much lower percentage of Arabic words in colloquial forms of the language."

This is demonstrated by the mediocre linguistic contribution, for which exhaustive statistical studies have been made (Doerfer). The number of Turkish or Mongol words that entered Persian, though not negligible, remained limited to 2,135, i.e., 3 percent of the vocabulary at the most. These new words are confined on the one hand to the military and political sector (titles, administration, etc.) and, on the other hand, to technical pastoral terms. The contrast with Arab influence is striking. While cultural pressure of the Arabs on Iran had been intense, they in no way infringed upon the entire Iranian territory, whereas with the Turks, whose contributions to Iranian civilization were modest, vast regions of Iranian lands were assimilated, notwithstanding the fact that resistance by the latter was ultimately victorious.
